ground_truth.description: ISS-inspired slow coolant leak signature on the AMS branch of the EDEN ISS Thermal Control System. Pressure decays sub-threshold for the first hour then accelerates; coupled inlet/outlet temperatures drift upward after the controller's compensation lag; the loop valve opens stepwise as the controller reacts. Reproduces the *signature pattern* of the ISS P1 EATCS ammonia leak (NTRS 20190029027, NTRS 20220003097) — not its physics.

failure_mode.name: Synthetic NDS Pump/Circulation Anomaly Archetype Inspired by EDEN ISS Telemetry
failure_mode.typical_onset: hypothesized — gradual increase in oscillation amplitude on the loop pressure and EC sensors over days to weeks; intermittent at first, then sustained
distinguishing_features: oscillation appears simultaneously on pressure and EC sensors of the same NDS loop (multi-sensor, not single-threshold-breach) — the kind of signature unsupervised detectors like DAMP/MDI are well-suited for
tank level may co-drift over a longer horizon, but the cause of any tank-level drift is not established by the source paper and could equally reflect dosing, evaporation, sampling, or sensor effects
PH excursions may track the EC oscillation; the source paper does not establish the mechanism, so this is best treated as a coupling hypothesis rather than a confirmed cascade

the EDEN ISS NDS sub-system instruments two tanks, with redundant EC and PH probes per tank (e.g. ec-tank1_1 and ec-tank1_2) — within-tank probe agreement is the cleanest way to separate a real loop event from single-probe drift
failure_mode.historical_context:
This entry is a hypothesized engineering archetype, not a documented historical EDEN ISS
failure case. Provenance disclosure:

Directly supported by the source material:
- The EDEN ISS NDS subsystem instruments pressure (8 sensors), electrical conductivity (4),
  pH (4), temperature (4), tank level (2), and tank volume (2) — verified against the
  Zenodo dataset metadata (record 11485183).
- Rewicki et al. (2024, https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.09825) apply unsupervised anomaly

  on-site spares, manual bleed-and-reprime procedures) is not sourced from the paper or
  from any EDEN ISS operations log cited here, and has been removed.

No operator playbook is documented for this pattern in the source paper. The following are

(1) compare redundant within-tank probes (ec-tank*_1 vs ec-tank*_2; ph-tank*_1 vs

failure_mode.name: EDEN ISS AMS CO2 Baseline Drift (synthetic interpretation of Rewicki et al. anomaly classes)
failure_mode.typical_onset: gradual rise in baseline CO2 concentration over days to weeks while the diurnal photosynthesis cycle remains intact
distinguishing_features: the diurnal CO2 oscillation amplitude (drawdown during illumination, recovery during dark) is preserved — only the baseline shifts upward
relative humidity may co-drift in the same window (paper anomaly candidate "Temperature + CO2 Peak with RH Drop" and "Level Shift")
VPD (vapor pressure deficit) follows from temperature × RH and may shift even if temperature is steady
the pattern is a baseline shift rather than a peak or transient — distinguishing it from the paper's "CO2 Peak" anomaly candidate even though both involve the same sensors
failure_mode.historical_context:
This entry is a synthetic but physically plausible scenario derived from the recurring
multivariate AMS anomaly patterns described in Rewicki et al. (2024,
https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.09825). The paper documents three concrete anomaly candidate
classes on the AMS-SES sensors — "CO2 Peak", "Temperature + CO2 Peak with RH Drop", and
"Level Shift" — and explicitly declines to assign confirmed root causes, stating that the
authors "lack evidence to assert consistent underlying causes across instances." This KB
entry takes the "Level Shift" pattern (a sustained baseline rise rather than a peak) and
treats it as a hypothesized scenario class for the agent to reason about. No EDEN ISS
operator log, maintenance record, or scrubber service event is cited in support of the
scenario; the entry exists to give the agent a plausible non-thermal anomaly archetype to
match against during diagnosis, with the scrubber-degradation hypothesis listed as one of

failure_mode.name: Synthetic Illumination Degradation Scenario Inspired by EDEN ISS Telemetry
failure_mode.typical_onset: hypothesized gradual reduction in delivered PAR over weeks to months, with the daily on/off cycle envelope intact but the illuminated-phase plateau lower than baseline
distinguishing_features: drift is on the on-phase plateau height, not on the rise/fall edges or the off-phase floor — detection must compare same-phase samples rather than rolling averages, since the daily on/off cycle dominates the raw signal
PAR is measured by a small number of sensors (3 in the EDEN ISS dataset, all in AMS-FEG/AMS-SES rather than ICS), so per-panel uniformity cannot be observed directly — only the array-level mean

this scenario is distinct from the paper-documented ICS anomaly classes (anomalous day phases, interrupted cooldowns, delayed warmups, missing day/night transitions), which appear on ICS temperature sensors as transient or step-like patterns rather than as a slow PAR plateau drift

the inferred coupling to CO2 (weakened photosynthetic drawdown raising the CO2 baseline) is physically plausible but not demonstrated in the source paper — treat it as a hypothesis to test against the data, not as an established cascade
failure_mode.historical_context:
This entry is a synthetic but physically plausible scenario, not a documented historical
failure case. The EDEN ISS telemetry dataset includes PAR (photosynthetically active
radiation) measurements in the Atmosphere Management System (AMS-FEG and AMS-SES) and
temperature telemetry associated with the Illumination Control System (ICS). Rewicki et
al. (2024, https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.09825) identify recurring anomalous operational
patterns in ICS telemetry — including anomalous day phases, interrupted cooldowns,
delayed warmups, and missing day/night transitions — and apply shape-based clustering to
isolate recurring anomalous behavior. The published work does not document confirmed LED
degradation events, panel aging failures, or maintenance/replacement actions, and
explicitly declines to assign root causes to its anomaly clusters. The scenario in this
entry constructs a hypothesized PAR-decline trajectory inspired by the dataset's sensor
composition; a gradual reduction in effective illumination would theoretically manifest
as declining PAR plateaus together with weakened photosynthetic CO2 drawdown, but that
causal chain is inferred rather than empirically established here.

failure_mode.name: ISS CDRA Capacity-Limited Cabin CO2 Rise (synthesized from NASA ECLSS background)
failure_mode.typical_onset: hours; cabin CO2 climbs when the metabolic generation rate exceeds the per-cycle removal capacity of the active CDRA configuration
distinguishing_features: the scenario hypothesis is a capacity limitation rather than a subsystem health fault — the scrubber subsystem itself may be operating nominally

the CO2 rise is on the timescale of crew activity (hours), not the timescale of a slow media degradation (days/weeks); this timescale separation is the cleanest discriminator from a scrubber-efficiency drift archetype

elevated cabin CO2 above the long-duration exposure limit is a known operational concern on ISS; the recovery trajectory after metabolic load drops or after a redundant stage is brought online is the candidate test for the capacity-limit hypothesis (recovery on demand → capacity-limited; no recovery → hardware fault more likely)

relative humidity coupling is physically plausible (desiccant drying is upstream of CO2 sorption in CDRA architecture) but is not verified to correlate reliably with capacity-limit events in any source cited here — treat any RH co-drift as supportive but not diagnostic
failure_mode.historical_context:
This entry is a synthesized scenario, not a documented incident report. Provenance
disclosure:

Directly supported by the cited sources:
- The ISS CDRA is a regenerable swing-bed sorbent CO2 removal assembly (NASA OCHMO ECLSS
  Technical Brief, 2023).
- Elevated cabin CO2 is a documented operational concern on ISS, with long-duration
  exposure limits in the low-single-digit mmHg range.
- CDRA capacity is affected by crew metabolic load, single-string operation during
  maintenance of a redundant assembly, and exercise activity blocks. These contributors
  are general background in NASA ECLSS material.

Inferred / not directly stated by the cited sources:
- "Bed saturation / overload" is plausible engineering shorthand but is not the canonical
  NASA fault terminology; NASA documents typically distinguish CDRA hardware faults,
  degraded performance, single-bed operation, sorbent/desiccant issues, and valve faults
  as separate categories.
- The previously asserted "cabin CO2 transfer function … characteristic asymmetric
  oscillation" was a synthesized diagnostic heuristic; no specific NASA telemetry
  signature in the cited sources establishes that asymmetric peaks-rise-faster-than-
  troughs-fall behavior is the operational marker of a capacity event. That claim has
  been removed.
- Reliable RH co-drift during capacity events is not confirmed in the cited sources; cabin
  humidity is influenced by condensate recovery, ventilation, crew activity, and thermal
  loops in addition to CDRA. Coupling is treated here as a hypothesis, not a feature.
- The "CDRA was designed for nominal four-crew metabolic CO2 load" framing has been
  removed: ISS life support has evolved over time and supports larger crews, and the
  cited sources do not anchor a specific nominal-crew design figure.
- The cited NASA OCHMO ECLSS brief is broad background material rather than direct
  evidence for the detailed phenomenology in this entry; the ISS daily summaries archive
  is a real archive but no specific entry is cited here. Both citations should be read as
  pointers to the source material category, not as proofs of any specific telemetry
  behavior described above.

The entry exists to give the agent a high-prior alternative hypothesis to weigh against
a slow scrubber-degradation diagnosis when cabin CO2 rises on the timescale of hours.

failure_mode.name: ISS Port 1 EATCS Ammonia Leak (Loop A, 2011–2013)
failure_mode.typical_onset: gradual over weeks to years; decay rate accelerates as mechanical damage at the leak site progresses
distinguishing_features: decay rate of the loop pressure accelerates over time rather than decaying linearly

representative telemetry progression: pressure → loop quantity → flow control valve position → downstream temperatures (consistent with expected closed-loop thermal control behavior under progressive coolant loss; NASA sources directly cite pressure and inventory decay, the valve and temperature stages are engineering inferences)
leak is not localized by any single sensor; it is inferred from system-level mass-balance drift
working fluid (ammonia on EATCS) is not directly measured — only its bulk-state proxies are
failure_mode.historical_context:
A slow ammonia leak in the ISS Port 1 (P1) segment of the External Active Thermal Control
System (EATCS) Loop A was first operationally detected in 2011 through long-term loop
pressure and ammonia quantity telemetry trends. The leak rate gradually accelerated over
the following years; in May 2013 a sharp acceleration was visually confirmed by
crystallized ammonia flakes venting from the truss, and ISS flight controllers ordered an
unscheduled EVA on 2013-05-11 to swap the suspect Pump Flow Control Subassembly (PFCS).
The post-incident reviews (NTRS 20190029027, NTRS 20220003097) document the long-horizon
telemetry warning and the operationally relevant signatures (pressure decline, ammonia
inventory decay, accelerating leak rate). The cascade across pressure → quantity → valve
position → downstream temperature is presented in this entry as a representative telemetry
progression consistent with closed-loop thermal control behavior during progressive
coolant loss, not as a NASA-formulated canonical signature.

citations.title: The International Space Station (ISS) Port 1 (P1) External Active Thermal Control System (EATCS) Ammonia Leak (Cowan, Bond, Metcalf, 2019)
Coolant Leak from ISS External Active Thermal Control System (EATCS) - An Examination of Most Probable (Cowan et al., 2022)

failure_mode.typical_response:
Ground controllers tracked the trend over a long horizon rather than reacting to any single
sample. The standard playbook was: (1) confirm the trend against multiple correlated sensors
to rule out single-sensor calibration drift, (2) reconfigure loop interconnects to cross-feed
from the unaffected loop, (3) draw replenishment ammonia from the on-orbit reservoir while
modeling time-to-depletion, and (4) plan a contingency EVA when the projected depletion date
approached the EVA preparation lead time. The May 2013 EVA is the reference example of step 4
being executed under accelerated timeline pressure.

  oscillation" was a synthesized diagnostic heuristic; no specific NASA telemetry
  signature in the cited sources establishes that asymmetric peaks-rise-faster-than-
  troughs-fall behavior is the operational marker of a capacity event. That claim has
  been removed.

  humidity is influenced by condensate recovery, ventilation, crew activity, and thermal
  loops in addition to CDRA. Coupling is treated here as a hypothesis, not a feature.

  removed: ISS life support has evolved over time and supports larger crews, and the
  cited sources do not anchor a specific nominal-crew design figure.

failure_mode.historical_context:
A slow ammonia leak in the ISS Port 1 (P1) segment of the External Active Thermal Control
System (EATCS) Loop A was first operationally detected in 2011 through long-term loop
pressure and ammonia quantity telemetry trends. The leak rate gradually accelerated over
the following years; in May 2013 a sharp acceleration was visually confirmed by
crystallized ammonia flakes venting from the truss, and ISS flight controllers ordered an
unscheduled EVA on 2013-05-11 to swap the suspect Pump Flow Control Subassembly (PFCS).
The post-incident reviews (NTRS 20190029027, NTRS 20220003097) document the long-horizon
telemetry warning and the operationally relevant signatures (pressure decline, ammonia
inventory decay, accelerating leak rate). The cascade across pressure → quantity → valve
position → downstream temperature is presented in this entry as a representative telemetry
progression consistent with closed-loop thermal control behavior during progressive
coolant loss, not as a NASA-formulated canonical signature.

approached the EVA preparation lead time. The May 2013 EVA is the reference example of step 4
being executed under accelerated timeline pressure.

failure_mode.historical_context:
A slow ammonia leak in the ISS Port 1 (P1) segment of the External Active Thermal Control
System (EATCS) Loop A was first operationally detected in 2011 through long-term loop
pressure and ammonia quantity telemetry trends. The leak rate gradually accelerated over
the following years; in May 2013 a sharp acceleration was visually confirmed by
crystallized ammonia flakes venting from the truss, and ISS flight controllers ordered an
unscheduled EVA on 2013-05-11 to swap the suspect Pump Flow Control Subassembly (PFCS).
The post-incident reviews (NTRS 20190029027, NTRS 20220003097) document the long-horizon
telemetry warning and the operationally relevant signatures (pressure decline, ammonia
inventory decay, accelerating leak rate). The cascade across pressure → quantity → valve
position → downstream temperature is presented in this entry as a representative telemetry
progression consistent with closed-loop thermal control behavior during progressive
coolant loss, not as a NASA-formulated canonical signature.

from the unaffected loop, (3) draw replenishment ammonia from the on-orbit reservoir while
modeling time-to-depletion, and (4) plan a contingency EVA when the projected depletion date
approached the EVA preparation lead time. The May 2013 EVA is the reference example of step 4
being executed under accelerated timeline pressure.
